title: भगवद्गीता अध्याय १८ - मोक्षसन्न्यासयोग
titleEn: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 - Moksha Sannyasa Yoga
devanagariText:
॥ अथ अष्टादशोऽध्यायः - मोक्षसन्न्यासयोगः ॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
सन्न्यासस्य महाबाहो तत्त्वमिच्छामि वेदितुम्।
त्यागस्य च हृषीकेश पृथक्केशिनिषूदन॥१॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
काम्यानां कर्मणां न्यासं सन्न्यासं कवयो विदुः।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं प्राहुस्त्यागं विचक्षणाः॥२॥

त्याज्यं दोषवदित्येके कर्म प्राहुर्मनीषिणः।
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यमिति चापरे॥३॥

निश्चयं शृणु मे तत्र त्यागे भरतसत्तम।
त्यागो हि पुरुषव्याघ्र त्रिविधः सम्प्रकीर्तितः॥४॥

यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यं कार्यमेव तत्।
यज्ञो दानं तपश्चैव पावनानि मनीषिणाम्॥५॥

एतान्यपि तु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलानि च।
कर्तव्यानीति मे पार्थ निश्चितं मतमुत्तमम्॥६॥

नियतस्य तु सन्न्यासः कर्मणो नोपपद्यते।
मोहात्तस्य परित्यागस्तामसः परिकीर्तितः॥७॥

दुःखमित्येव यत्कर्म कायक्लेशभयात्त्यजेत्।
स कृत्वा राजसं त्यागं नैव त्यागफलं लभेत्॥८॥

कार्यमित्येव यत्कर्म नियतं क्रियतेऽर्जुन।
सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलं चैव स त्यागः सात्त्विको मतः॥९॥

न द्वेष्ट्यकुशलं कर्म कुशले नानुषज्जते।
त्यागी सत्त्वसमाविष्टो मेधावी छिन्नसंशयः॥१०॥

न हि देहभृता शक्यं त्यक्तुं कर्माण्यशेषतः।
यस्तु कर्मफलत्यागी स त्यागीत्यभिधीयते॥११॥

अनिष्टमिष्टं मिश्रं च त्रिविधं कर्मणः फलम्।
भवत्यत्यागिनां प्रेत्य न तु सन्न्यासिनां क्वचित्॥१२॥

पञ्चैतानि महाबाहो कारणानि निबोध मे।
साङ्ख्ये कृतान्ते प्रोक्तानि सिद्धये सर्वकर्मणाम्॥१३॥

अधिष्ठानं तथा कर्ता करणं च पृथग्विधम्।
विविधाश्च पृथक्चेष्टा दैवं चैवात्र पञ्चमम्॥१४॥

शरीरवाङ्मनोभिर्यत्कर्म प्रारभते नरः।
न्याय्यं वा विपरीतं वा पञ्चैते तस्य हेतवः॥१५॥

तत्रैवं सति कर्तारमात्मानं केवलं तु यः।
पश्यत्यकृतबुद्धित्वान्न स पश्यति दुर्मतिः॥१६॥

यस्य नाहङ्कृतो भावो बुद्धिर्यस्य न लिप्यते।
हत्वापि स इमाँल्लोकान्न हन्ति न निबध्यते॥१७॥

ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं परिज्ञाता त्रिविधा कर्मचोदना।
करणं कर्म कर्तेति त्रिविधः कर्मसङ्ग्रहः॥१८॥

सर्वभूतेषु येनैकं भावमव्ययमीक्षते।
अविभक्तं विभक्तेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि सात्त्विकम्॥२०॥

यत्तु कृत्स्नवदेकस्मिन्कार्ये सक्तमहैतुकम्।
अतत्त्वार्थवदल्पं च तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्॥२२॥

नियतं सङ्गरहितमरागद्वेषतः कृतम्।
अफलप्रेप्सुना कर्म यत्तत्सात्त्विकमुच्यते॥२३॥

यत्तु कामेप्सुना कर्म साहङ्कारेण वा पुनः।
क्रियते बहुलायासं तद्राजसमुदाहृतम्॥२४॥

सिद्धिं प्राप्तो यथा ब्रह्म तथाप्नोति निबोध मे।
समासेनैव कौन्तेय निष्ठा ज्ञानस्य या परा॥५०॥

बुद्ध्या विशुद्धया युक्तो धृत्यात्मानं नियम्य च।
शब्दादीन्विषयांस्त्यक्त्वा रागद्वेषौ व्युदस्य च॥५१॥

विविक्तसेवी लघ्वाशी यतवाक्कायमानसः।
ध्यानयोगपरो नित्यं वैराग्यं समुपाश्रितः॥५२॥

अहङ्कारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं परिग्रहम्।
विमुच्य निर्ममः शान्तो ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते॥५३॥

ब्रह्मभूतः प्रसन्नात्मा न शोचति न काङ्क्षति।
समः सर्वेषु भूतेषु मद्भक्तिं लभते पराम्॥५४॥

भक्त्या मामभिजानाति यावान्यश्चास्मि तत्त्वतः।
ततो मां तत्त्वतो ज्ञात्वा विशते तदनन्तरम्॥५५॥

सर्वकर्माण्यपि सदा कुर्वाणो मद्व्यपाश्रयः।
मत्प्रसादादवाप्नोति शाश्वतं पदमव्ययम्॥५६॥

चेतसा सर्वकर्माणि मयि सन्न्यस्य मत्परः।
बुद्धियोगमुपाश्रित्य मच्चित्तः सततं भव॥५७॥

मच्चित्तः सर्वदुर्गाणि मत्प्रसादात्तरिष्यसि।
अथ चेत्त्वमहङ्कारान्न श्रोष्यसि विनङ्क्ष्यसि॥५८॥

सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज।
अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः॥६६॥

इदं ते नातपस्काय नाभक्ताय कदाचन।
न चाशुश्रूषवे वाच्यं न च मां योऽभ्यसूयति॥६७॥

य इमं परमं गुह्यं मद्भक्तेष्वभिधास्यति।
भक्तिं मयि परां कृत्वा मामेवैष्यत्यसंशयः॥६८॥

न च तस्मान्मनुष्येषु कश्चिन्मे प्रियकृत्तमः।
भविता न च मे तस्मादन्यः प्रियतरो भुवि॥६९॥

अध्येष्यते च य इमं धर्म्यं संवादमावयोः।
ज्ञानयज्ञेन तेनाहमिष्टः स्यामिति मे मतिः॥७०॥

श्रद्धावाननसूयश्च शृणुयादपि यो नरः।
सोऽपि मुक्तः शुभाँल्लोकान्प्राप्नुयात्पुण्यकर्मणाम्॥७१॥

कच्चिदेतच्छ्रुतं पार्थ त्वयैकाग्रेण चेतसा।
कच्चिदज्ञानसम्मोहः प्रनष्टस्ते धनञ्जय॥७२॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
नष्टो मोहः स्मृतिर्लब्धा त्वत्प्रसादान्मयाच्युत।
स्थितोऽस्मि गतसन्देहः करिष्ये वचनं तव॥७३॥

सञ्जय उवाच —
इत्यहं वासुदेवस्य पार्थस्य च महात्मनः।
संवादमिममश्रौषमद्भुतं रोमहर्षणम्॥७४॥

व्यासप्रसादाच्छ्रुतवानेतद्गुह्यमहं परम्।
योगं योगेश्वरात्कृष्णात्साक्षात्कथयतः स्वयम्॥७५॥

राजन्संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य संवादमिममद्भुतम्।
केशवार्जुनयोः पुण्यं हृष्यामि च मुहुर्मुहुः॥७६॥

तच्च संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य रूपमत्यद्भुतं हरेः।
विस्मयो मे महान्राजन्हृष्यामि च पुनः पुनः॥७७॥

यत्र योगेश्वरः कृष्णो यत्र पार्थो धनुर्धरः।
तत्र श्रीर्विजयो भूतिर्ध्रुवा नीतिर्मतिर्मम॥७८॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे
श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे मोक्षसन्न्यासयोगो नाम अष्टादशोऽध्यायः ॥
transliteration:
|| Atha Ashtadasho'dhyayah - Moksha Sannyasa Yogah ||

Arjuna Uvacha —
Sannyasasya Maha-Baho Tattvam-Ichchhami Veditum.
Tyagasya Cha Hrishikesha Prithak-Keshi-Nishudana.||1||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Kamyanam Karmanam Nyasam Sannyasam Kavayo Viduh.
Sarva-Karma-Phala-Tyagam Prahus-Tyagam Vichakshanah.||2||

Yajna-Dana-Tapah-Karma Na Tyajyam Karyam-Eva Tat.
Yajno Danam Tapash-Chaiva Pavanani Manishinam.||5||

Etany-Api Tu Karmani Sangam Tyaktva Phalani Cha.
Kartavyani-Iti Me Partha Nishchitam Matam-Uttamam.||6||

Karyam-Ity-Eva Yat-Karma Niyatam Kriyate'rjuna.
Sangam Tyaktva Phalam Chaiva Sa Tyagah Sattviko Matah.||9||

Sarva-Bhuteshu Yena-Ikam Bhavam-Avyayam-Ikshate.
Avibhaktam Vibhakteshu Taj-Jnanam Viddhi Sattvikam.||20||

Buddhya Vishuddhaya Yukto Dhritya-Atmanam Niyamya Cha.
Shabdadin Vishayams-Tyaktva Raga-Dveshau Vyudasya Cha.||51||

Brahma-Bhutah Prasanna-Atma Na Shochati Na Kankshati.
Samah Sarveshu Bhuteshu Mad-Bhaktim Labhate Param.||54||

Sarva-Dharmaan Parityajya Mam-Ekam Sharanam Vraja.
Aham Tva Sarva-Papebhyo Mokshayishyami Ma Shuchah.||66||

Arjuna Uvacha —
Nashto Mohah Smritir-Labdha Tvat-Prasaadan-Maya-Achyuta.
Sthito'smi Gata-Sandehah Karishye Vachanam Tava.||73||

Sanjaya Uvacha —
Yatra Yogeshvarah Krishno Yatra Partho Dhanur-Dharah.
Tatra Shrir-Vijayo Bhutir-Dhruva Nitir-Matir-Mama.||78||

|| Iti Shrimad-Bhagavad-Gitasu-Upanishatsu Brahma-Vidyayam Yoga-Shastre
Shri-Krishna-Arjuna-Samvade Moksha-Sannyasa-Yogo Nama Ashtadasho'dhyayah ||
hindiMeaning: अष्टादश अध्याय 'मोक्षसन्न्यासयोग' गीता का समापन अध्याय है और सबसे बड़ा अध्याय है। अर्जुन सन्न्यास और त्याग का भेद पूछते हैं। कृष्ण कहते हैं — काम्य कर्मों का त्याग सन्न्यास है, सब कर्मफलों का त्याग 'त्याग' है। यज्ञ, दान और तप कभी न छोड़ें — ये पवित्र करने वाले हैं, पर फल और आसक्ति छोड़कर करें। ज्ञान, कर्म, कर्ता, बुद्धि, धृति, सुख — सबका तीन गुणों के अनुसार वर्गीकरण है। फिर वर्णधर्म और स्वधर्म पालन का महत्व बताया। अंत में गीता का चरम उपदेश — 'सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज। अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः' — सब धर्मों को छोड़कर मेरी शरण में आ, मैं तुझे सब पापों से मुक्त करूँगा, शोक मत कर। अर्जुन कहते हैं — मेरा मोह नष्ट हो गया, स्मृति प्राप्त हुई, मैं आपका वचन करूँगा। संजय कहते हैं — जहाँ योगेश्वर कृष्ण हैं और जहाँ धनुर्धर अर्जुन हैं, वहीं श्री, विजय, भूति और नीति है।
benefits: Contains the supreme verse: Sarva Dharman Parityajya Mam Ekam Sharanam Vraja
Provides the complete conclusion and summary of all Gita teachings
Teaches the highest form of surrender and divine grace
Classifies knowledge, action, and actors by the three Gunas
Assures that God will free the surrendered soul from all sins
Ends with the victory declaration: where Krishna and Arjuna are, there is prosperity
Invokes supreme peace, liberation, and fulfillment of life's purpose
description: Dedicated to Lord Krishna, the Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 - Moksha Sannyasa Yoga (भगवद्गीता अध्याय १८ - मोक्षसन्न्यासयोग) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. This sacred composition is sourced from Bhagavad Gita - Mahabharata and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Regular recitation is believed to teach the highest form of surrender and divine grace, grant supreme peace, liberation, and fulfillment of life's purpose, and provide the complete conclusion and summary of all Gita teachings. For best results, devotees chant this on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami. The text contains 78 verses and takes around 35 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 - Moksha Sannyasa Yoga in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 — Moksha Sannyasa Yoga?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 (भगवद्गीता अध्याय १८), titled Moksha Sannyasa Yoga (the Yoga of Liberation by Renunciation), is the final and longest chapter of the Srimad Bhagavad Gita — containing 78 verses. It is the grand summary and conclusion of the entire Gita: Krishna recapitulates and synthesises every major teaching of the previous seventeen chapters, distinguishes sannyasa (renunciation of action) from tyaga (renunciation of the fruits of action), gives the threefold guna-classification of jnana, karma, karta, buddhi, dhriti and sukha, describes the sva-dharma and svabhava-karma of the four varnas, the path of the karma-yogi to brahmi-sthiti, the four steps of bhakti-yoga, and culminates in the supreme charama-shloka (verse 18.66) — Sarva-dharman parityajya mam-ekam sharanam vraja — the complete teaching of saranagati. The chapter ends with Sanjaya's celebrated declaration that wherever Krishna and Arjuna are, there is victory, prosperity and unfailing morality (verses 18.74-78). (Source: Srimad Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18; Mahabharata Bhishma Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between sannyasa and tyaga in Chapter 18?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 18.1-18.12 open with Arjuna's question on sannyasa versus tyaga. Krishna explains: (1) Sannyasa — the renunciation (nyasa) of all desire-prompted actions (kamya-karmas); the wise know it as sannyasa. (2) Tyaga — the relinquishment (parityaga) of the fruit (phala) of all action; the wise call this tyaga. Some philosophers say all action should be given up as evil; others say sacrifice, charity and austerity should never be relinquished. Krishna gives his own conclusion (verse 18.6): Etany-api tu karmani sangam tyaktva phalani cha, kartavyaniti me Partha nishchitam matam-uttamam — These works (yajna, dana, tapas) should be performed but with detachment and abandonment of the fruit; this, O Partha, is My settled and supreme conclusion. Sannyasa with delusion (refusal to act) is tamasic; with fear of bodily trouble, rajasic; performance of duty with abandonment of attachment and fruit is sattvic-tyaga. The chapter thereby reconciles the apparent conflict between the path of action (Karma Yoga) and the path of renunciation (Sannyasa Yoga). (Source: Bhagavad Gita 18.1-18.12.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous charama-shloka — Sarva-dharman parityajya — in Chapter 18?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 18.66 is the supreme charama-shloka (final verse) of the Bhagavad Gita: Sarva-dharman parityajya mam-ekam sharanam vraja, aham tva sarva-papebhyo mokshayishyami ma shuchah — Abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in Me alone; I shall liberate you from all sins; do not grieve. This single verse is the foundational pramana of the Sri Vaishnava sampradaya's doctrine of sharanagati (complete surrender), the Madhva doctrine of bhagavad-prapatti, and the Gaudiya Vaishnava doctrine of sharanapatti. Acharya Ramanuja called it the charama-shloka — the ultimate verse — and his entire Gita Bhashya culminates in its exposition. The verse teaches that beyond all paths of dharma (karma, jnana, dhyana, yajna, dana, tapas) there is one supreme way — total surrender to the personal Lord, who personally takes responsibility for liberating the devotee from all karmic burden. The verse is the moksha-mantra of countless Vaishnavas. (Sources: Bhagavad Gita 18.66; Sri Bhashya; Gita Bhashyas of all sampradayas.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the five causes of action (panch-karana) taught in Chapter 18?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 18.13-18.16 give the Sankhya-based teaching of the five causes of all action: (1) Adhishthana — the body or seat (the locus of action), (2) Karta — the agent or doer (the embodied self with ego), (3) Karana — the various instruments (the eleven indriyas including the mind), (4) Cheshta — the various efforts (the diverse motions of prana, vital energies and limbs), and (5) Daiva — the divine factor (the unseen factor — past karma, divine grace and cosmic forces). Whatever action a person performs by body, speech or mind — whether right or wrong — these five are its causes. Krishna teaches (18.17) that one who has no sense of doership (yasya naham-krito bhavah) and whose buddhi is unstained — even if he kills these warriors, he does not really kill, nor is he bound by the act. This is one of the most profound teachings on agency and karma in all Hindu philosophy and prepares the ground for the surrender of 18.66. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 18.13-18.17.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the threefold guna-classification of knowledge, action, agent, intellect, will and happiness in Chapter 18?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 18.18-18.40 systematically classify into sattvic-rajasic-tamasic types: (1) Jnana (knowledge) — sattvic sees the one undivided in all beings; rajasic sees many separate; tamasic clings to one effect as the whole. (2) Karma (action) — sattvic without attachment, without like or dislike, without desire for fruit; rajasic with desire and ego; tamasic regardless of consequence, loss, injury or capacity. (3) Karta (agent) — sattvic free from attachment and ego, full of zeal and equanimity; rajasic passionate, greedy, cruel; tamasic unsteady, vulgar, deceitful, lazy. (4) Buddhi (intellect) — sattvic knows when to act and refrain, what is fear and freedom from fear, bondage and liberation; rajasic confuses dharma and adharma; tamasic mistakes adharma for dharma. (5) Dhriti (firmness) — sattvic firm in dharana of mind, prana and senses through yoga; rajasic firm in dharma, kama, artha for fruit; tamasic firm in foolish persistence in sleep, fear, grief, depression and pride. (6) Sukha (happiness) — sattvic begins like poison and ends like nectar (born of self-knowledge); rajasic begins like nectar and ends like poison (born of sense-objects); tamasic delusive from start to finish (born of sleep, sloth and heedlessness). (Source: Bhagavad Gita 18.18-18.40.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Chapter 18 teach about svadharma and the four varnas?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 18.41-18.48 give the celebrated teaching on svadharma and varna-svabhava-karma: The duties of brahmins, kshatriyas, vaishyas and shudras are distributed according to the gunas born of their svabhava (own innate nature). Brahmin's nature-born works are sama, dama, tapa, shaucha, kshanti, arjava, jnana, vijnana and astikya (faith). Kshatriya's are valour (shaurya), splendour (tejas), firmness (dhriti), skill (dakshya), not fleeing from battle, generosity, lordliness. Vaishya's are krishi (agriculture), gorakshya (cattle-protection), and vanijya (trade). Shudra's nature-born work is paricharyatmaka (service of all). Verse 18.45 — Sve sve karmany-abhiratah samsiddhim labhate narah — A person devoted to one's own duty attains supreme perfection. Verse 18.47 — Shreyan svadharmo vigunah para-dharmat sv-anushthitat — Better is one's own dharma though imperfect than another's dharma well-performed. Verse 18.48: Saha-jam karma Kaunteya sa-dosham-api na tyajet — Inherent duty, even if defective, should not be abandoned, for all undertakings are enveloped in defect as fire is by smoke. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 18.41-18.48.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous closing verse Yatra Yogeshvarah Krishna in Chapter 18?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 18.78, Sanjaya's closing declaration, is one of the most quoted and beloved verses of the Bhagavad Gita: Yatra Yogeshvarah Krishno yatra Partho dhanur-dharah, tatra shrir vijayo bhutir-dhruva nitir-matir-mama — Wherever there is Krishna, the Lord of Yoga, and wherever there is Partha (Arjuna), the wielder of the bow, there shall be prosperity (shri), victory (vijaya), happiness (bhuti), and unfailing morality (dhruva niti) — this is my conviction. This verse is recited at the beginning of every Hindu auspicious undertaking (mangalacharana), at the start of every business venture, before a marriage, before journeys, and as a daily mangala-shloka. It is the final word of the entire 700-verse Gita and the final word of the entire Bhishma Parva. The combination — God in his Lord-of-Yoga form (jnana-shakti) plus the devoted disciple wielding the bow (karma-shakti) — is the formula for all spiritual and worldly success. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 18.78.)

seoFaqs.question: When is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Chapter 18 is recited on Day 18 of an 18-day Gita Parayana cycle — the supreme culminating day, often performed on Gita Jayanti itself (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi — Mokshada Ekadashi). It is also chanted on: Wednesday (Budhvar — Krishna's day), Janmashtami, Vaikuntha Ekadashi (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi in the South — the same day as Mokshada Ekadashi), every Ekadashi, and at the bedside of the dying (because the charama-shloka 18.66 is the supreme moksha-mantra). Many sadhakas recite Chapter 18 monthly on Krishna's birth-tithi or weekly on Wednesday. The chapter is the supreme protection mantra (verse 18.78), the supreme victory mantra (Yatra Yogeshvarah Krishna), the supreme moksha-shloka (18.66), and the supreme summary of Vedanta. The Gita Mahatmya in the Padma Purana declares that one who recites Chapter 18 attains the merit of reciting the entire Gita. (Sources: Padma Purana Gita Mahatmya; Mokshada Ekadashi Vrat Katha; classical Vaishnavite tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Chapter 18 connect to the verse-by-verse Gita pages on this site?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 18 — Moksha Sannyasa Yoga contains 78 verses, each studied verse-by-verse on this site at /gita/chapter-18/verse-1 through /gita/chapter-18/verse-78. The verse-level pages provide the Devanagari, transliteration, word-by-word meaning, English translation, Hindi anvaya and commentary by classical acharyas (Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva, Gyaneshwar) for each shloka — particularly important for the panch-karana verses (18.13-17), the comprehensive guna-classifications (18.18-40), the svadharma teaching (18.41-48), the four-step bhakti culmination (18.51-65), the supreme charama-shloka (18.66), the Gita-pathana phalashruti (18.68-72), and the closing Sanjaya verses (18.74-78). This stotra page presents the entire chapter as a single text for parayana — the supreme culmination of the eighteen-day Gita Parayana cycle and the most-recited single chapter for moksha-darshan. Together with Chapter 12, Chapter 15 and Chapter 2, it forms the four pillar-chapters of daily Gita practice. (Source: Stotra and Gita parayana traditions.)
